items.title: Why Elon Musk interferes in politics around the world | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Tech billionaire Elon Musk is planning a live online discussion next week with the candidate for chancellor of the far-right Alternative for Germany party, Alice Weidel.

The chat is expected to take place on Musk's platform X. It is his latest endorsement of the AfD ahead of Germany's general election on February 23rd. Last weekend, Musk caused outrage when he described the far-right party as the country's "last spark of hope" in an opinion piece in a German newspaper. Political leaders in Germany have accused him of election interference, considering his close relationship with US President-elect Donald Trump. 

00:00 Elon Musk plans onlince discussion with AfD leader
00:42 Jonathan Katz, Anti-Corruption, Democracy and Security Project at the Brookings Institution

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: End of Russia-Ukraine gas deal: Will Germany fall back on Russian gas after the election? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The European Union says gas supplies are stable after deliveries of Russian gas via Ukraine ended on New Year's Day. Russian gas accounted for less than 10 percent of the European Union's gas imports in 2023, but some eastern members – including Slovakia and Hungary – were still heavily relying on them.

Just outside of the EU, the effects of the gas cuts were immediately felt in Moldova and especially its Russian-backed breakaway region of Transnistria, where heating and hot water supplies to households were cut off. Ukraine stopped the transit after a decade-long contract with Moscow expired. Kyiv said not renewing the deal was in the interests of national security, and a way to hurt Russia's economy. 

00:00 EU says supplies are stable after Ukraine cut Russian gas
00:50 Benjamin Schmitt, senior fellow at the University of Pennsylvania's Center for Energy Policy 

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: How Assad used Sednaya prison to prop up his regime | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The Sednaya Prison, north of Syria’s capital, Damascus, was one of the first places liberated by the rebels. Human rights observers estimate 30,000 detainees were killed there since the beginning of the Syrian civil war in 2011. Many others were tortured. 

Some who survived have been reunited with friends and family after being detained for years. For our report, DW’s Aya Ibrahim and Rama Jarmakani in Damascus have been talking to a former detainee. 

00:00 Inside Sednaya Prison
04:14 Joseph Braude, President of the Center for Peace Communications
12:07 Omar Alshogre, Human rights advocate with the Syrian Emergency Task Force

items.title: Nippon Steel: Why did Biden block a $15B Japanese takeover of US steel? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Incumbent President Joe Biden has blocked a $15 billion takeover of US Steel by Japan's Nippon Steel. 

Biden said it was necessary to protect American industry and prevent a key national asset from coming under foreign control. Both Biden and President-elect Donald Trump also opposed the takeover to protect jobs. Nippon Steel is to challenge the decision. The White House says it's not a slight against Japan, and is aimed at keeping steel production in the US.

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: French, German FMs meet Syria's new de facto leaders | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Germany's foreign minister Annalena Baerbock and her counterpart, France's Jean-Noel Barrot, are the first ministers from the EU to visit Syria and meet with de facto leader Ahmad al-Sharaa since rebels seized control of Damascus on December 8, 2024. Their visit to Syria is the highest-level western visit since the fall of the Assad regime. 

They expresseed support for the rebuilding of Syria. But they also made it clear that the EU will not finance Islamism - or tolerate discrimination against women or ethnic and religious groups.

DW talks to Christoph Schult in Damascus who is currently traveling with FM Baerbock. He is a journalist at Germany's Spiegel magazine.

There's some controversy over the Syrian leader's greeting for his EU visitors. Ahmad al-Sharaa shook hands with the French foreign minister Jean-Noel Barrot, a man, but not with Gemany's Foreign Minister Annalena Baerbock, a woman. 

00:00 EU extends cautious olive branch to Syria
02:38 Christoph Schult in Damascus 
08:04 Anchal Vora, DW Correspondent in Brussels
08:09 What signal is Syria's new leader sending?
13:02 Giorgio Cafiero, Gulf State Analytics  

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: The American Dream has become "inaccessible and unaffordable," says first trans congresswoman
items.author: DW News
items.content:
Sarah McBride is making history yet again as the first openly transgender congresswoman-elect in the US. The Democrat first made history as the US' first openly transgender state senator of Delaware.  
 
In an interview with DW's Washington Bureau Chief Ines Pohl, she talks about the American Dream and as pillars of that dream, she prioritizes bringing down the cost of housing, child care and health care. She expects serious Republicans to collaborate with Democrats to ensure the federal government progresses and "work across disagreement."

items.title: BSW: The new party disrupting German politics | Berlin Briefing Podcast
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The “Bündnis Sahra Wagenknecht” party or BSW is scrambling definitions of left and right ahead of Germany’s upcoming elections. Its leader Sahra Wagenknecht stems from the far left, but now accuses her former allies of being obsessed with identity politics rather than the interests of the working class. Her party is running on a mix of policies with both left-wing and right-wing characteristics. 

In this episode of Berlin Briefing, DW’s Richard Walker and Matthew Moore break down the party’s positions together with German historian Katja Hoyer. From the BSW’s opposition to NATO, climate policies, and sending weapons to Ukraine, to its call for a stronger welfare state and a revival of Germany’s traditional industries with energy from Russia – the team build up a picture of this taboo-busting party and end with predictions about its impact on the political landscape.
_
DW’s Matthew Moore: BSW profile with Sahra Wagenknecht interview
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vKOhG4R0pq8

items.title: Impeached president Yoon's security and supporters block his arrest I DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
South Korean investigators have suspended their efforts to arrest impeached President Yoon Suk Yeol for insurrection over his short-lived martial law decree last month. The decision follows a standoff with security services and with hundreds of Yoon's supporters gathered outside his residence in the South Korean capital, vowing to block his arrest. Yoon's lawyers called the arrest warrant 'invalid' and 'illegal'.

items.title: German foreign minister makes surprise visit to Syria I DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
German Foreign Minister Annalena Baerbock has arrived in Syria, following former leader Bashar al-Assad's removal from power. 

She and her French counterpart Jean-Noël Barrot will represent the European Union in talks with the new transitional government and its Islamist rebel leader Ahmed al-Sharaa. Baerbock is expected to outline the EU's conditions for resuming relations with Syria.

items.title: Why does the FBI now say the New Orleans attacker acted alone? I DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
The FBI says the man who commited the New Year's terror attack in New Orleans acted alone. 

Fourteen people were killed and dozens more injured when army veteran Shamsud Din-Jabbar rammed a truck into crowds of revellers in the city's French Quarter. The Texan is said to have pledged alliegance to the so-called Islamic State, and had made recordings in which he condemned music, drugs and alcohol. He was killed in a shoot-out with police after the attack. 

Meanwhile activities in New Orleans are resuming less than two days after the tragic event - and the famous street where it happened has been re-opened.

items.title: South Korea martial law scandal: Investigators to arrest impeached president Yoon Suk Yeol | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
In Seoul, South Korean anti-corruption investigators are trying to arrest impeached President Yoon Suk Yeol over his short-lived martial law decree last month.

Hundreds of Yoon's supporters have gathered outside his residence in the South Korean capital, vowing to block his arrest. It's unclear if Yoon himself will cooperate, his lawyers have called the arrest warrant 'invalid' and 'illegal'. Yoon is under criminal investigation for insurrection and is already suspended from office over his attempt to impose martial law.
